articles.title: US: Student visa issuances fell by -36% in summer 2025; OPT uncertainty among factors affecting international student demand
articles.summary: US student visa issuances fell by 36% in summer 2025, with uncertainty surrounding the Optional Practical Training (OPT) programme cited as a key factor affecting international student demand. The US government has renewed its focus on the OPT programme, and former Secretary of Homeland Security Kristi Noem, who was replaced on 5 March, had previously responded favourably to Senator Eric…
articles.insight:
The 36% drop in US student visa issuances during summer 2025—linked partly to OPT uncertainty—suggests growing caution among international applicants, though it remains unclear whether this reflects temporary policy anxiety or a deeper, sustained shift in destination preferences across key source markets.  
Admissions teams should track real-time application and enrolment trends by region and programme level, while closely monitoring US regulatory developments around OPT, visa processing timelines, and comparative messaging from peer destinations to assess evolving competitive positioning.

articles.title: New Zealand expands post-study work opportunities for international students
articles.summary: New Zealand plans to introduce a new Short Term Graduate Work Visa and extend eligibility for the Post Study Work Visa, with implementation scheduled for late 2026. No specific launch date has been confirmed, and the government’s international education strategy emphasizes managed growth over the coming years.
articles.insight:
New Zealand’s planned 2026 visa reforms signal a strategic effort to retain talent post-study, potentially strengthening its appeal—especially among students from Asia and the Pacific—though actual demand shifts depend on implementation details and concurrent policy changes elsewhere.  
Admissions teams should track New Zealand’s final visa criteria, regional marketing responses, and comparative post-study work offerings in Australia, the UK, and Canada, as these will shape student decision-making amid tightening global mobility pathways.

articles.title: Study Abroad in NZ: Visa length linked to qualification level and course duration, all you need to know
articles.summary: New Zealand’s Post Study Work Visa allows international graduates to stay and work for up to three years, with the visa length determined by both the qualification level and the official duration of the completed course. Visa validity is fixed based on the original offer of place and approved academic programme, and delays in completing studies do not extend the visa duration.
articles.insight:
This news reinforces New Zealand’s competitive post-study work offer, potentially influencing student choice—especially among qualification-conscious applicants from Asia and Latin America—though direct demand shifts remain uncertain without parallel data on enrolment trends or comparative policy changes elsewhere.  
Admissions teams should monitor NZ’s graduate employment outcomes and visa uptake rates, while reviewing how their own post-study work pathways are positioned in marketing—particularly for master’s and vocational programmes where duration-qualification alignment affects eligibility.

articles.title: British Council offers masters scholarships in the UK exclusively for women
articles.summary: The British Council has opened applications for full master’s scholarships exclusively for women in science, technology, engineering, and mathematics (STEM). These scholarships are offered through the Women in STEM program and provide places at the University of Warwick and Aston University in England.
articles.insight:
This initiative signals growing institutional emphasis on gender equity in STEM recruitment, potentially influencing international students’ perceptions of inclusivity and opportunity—though its direct impact on overall demand remains uncertain without data on uptake or spillover effects to other destinations.  
Admissions teams should monitor whether similar targeted scholarships emerge in their jurisdictions and assess if partner agents or high-achieving female applicants increasingly prioritize equity-focused offerings when comparing destination countries or institutions.

articles.title: Hong Kong to establish global version of university entrance exam on pilot basis
articles.summary: Hong Kong’s education authorities plan to pilot an internationally benchmarked version of the city’s Diploma of Secondary Education (DSE) exam to enable foreign students to apply for admission to higher education institutions in Hong Kong and mainland China. Technical explorations and studies with relevant bodies on developing this international DSE version are currently underway, according to Secretary for Education Christine Choi Yuk-lin.
articles.insight:
Hong Kong’s pilot international DSE may gradually diversify global student pathways to Chinese-speaking higher education, potentially affecting demand for Anglophone degrees—though its scale, recognition, and uptake by foreign students remain uncertain and likely years from material impact.  
Admissions teams should monitor the pilot’s rollout timeline, participating institutions, and accreditation outcomes, particularly how mainland Chinese universities adopt it, as early signals could inform regional recruitment positioning and articulation pathway reviews.

articles.title: European Math Prize winner joins Fields Medalist Ngo Bao Chau in initiative to curb Vietnam brain drain
articles.summary: Phan Thanh Nam, the first Vietnamese national to win the European Mathematical Society (EMS) Prize, is one of six world-class mathematicians who have agreed to regularly return to Vietnam to mentor doctoral students. This initiative was launched at the invitation of Fields Medalist Ngo Bao Chau to help curb Vietnam’s brain drain.
articles.insight:
This news signals growing academic investment in Vietnam’s higher education ecosystem, potentially tempering outbound student mobility—though its impact on enrolment demand in English-speaking countries remains uncertain and likely gradual, given persistent preferences for global credentials and career pathways abroad.  
Admissions teams should monitor Vietnamese PhD applicant volumes and engagement with domestic research initiatives over the next 2–3 years, while assessing whether partnerships with Vietnamese universities or targeted scholarships could strengthen recruitment amid evolving talent retention efforts.

articles.title: Vietnamese teen with perfect 9.0 IELTS wins $205,000 scholarship to US tech institute
articles.summary: Nguyen Hoang Bao Ngoc, a Vietnamese teenager, achieved a perfect 9.0 score on the IELTS on her first attempt and scored 1,500 on the SAT. She received a full scholarship worth $204,860 to study computer science at the New Jersey Institute of Technology.
articles.insight:
This high-profile achievement signals growing academic competitiveness among Southeast Asian students, potentially reflecting improved English proficiency training and rising aspirations for elite STEM pathways—though it remains unclear whether such outliers indicate broader regional shifts in application volume or selectivity.  
Admissions teams should track IELTS/SAT score distributions and scholarship uptake trends across Vietnam and neighbouring markets, while reviewing how their outreach highlights merit-based financial support for high-achieving STEM candidates from underrepresented Southeast Asian backgrounds.
